1:
On Luke 16:1 — “There was a certain rich man, which had a steward; and the same was accused unto him that he had wasted his goods” — the witnesses open the parable of the unjust steward and its lesson on the use of wealth.

The Latin and Eastern fathers draw out the figure of stewardship. Pseudo-Chrysostom corrects a common error: we hold our goods “not as lords… but as guests and strangers”; Ambrose, “we are not ourselves the masters, but… the stewards of the property of others”; Theophylact, that to abuse the trust “to our own pleasures” makes us “guilty stewards.” Bede sets the parable in its place: after the three parables answering the murmuring Pharisees (Luke 15:2), the Lord adds this “on almsgiving,” for “almsgiving should be added after repentance.”

The grammatical-historical Robertson notes the parable is “given by Luke alone,” spoken to the disciples after the Pharisees’ self-righteousness was exposed; Ellicott warns that no parable “has been subjected to such various and discordant interpretations.” The Reformed Calvin states its “leading object”: “that we ought to deal kindly and generously with our neighbours, that… we may reap the fruit of our liberality” — warning against pressing “every minute circumstance.”

So the witnesses set the famous and difficult parable in its frame. The rich man is God, or the Lord of all; the steward is every man, entrusted with goods he does not own; the accusation of “wasting” is the charge against all who squander what God has lent. The fathers fasten on the governing truth — that we are stewards, not proprietors — and the Reformers on the parable’s aim, that the wise use of money is to lay it out in mercy against the day of reckoning. The witnesses thus open a teaching not on dishonesty but on foresight: as the worldly steward provided shrewdly for his future, so the children of light must use their entrusted wealth for the world to come.

So the witnesses set the parable in its frame. The fathers fasten on the governing truth — that we are stewards, not proprietors, of goods we do not own — and the Reformers on its aim, that the wise use of money is to lay it out in mercy against the day of reckoning. Bede notes its place after the parables of repentance: almsgiving follows repentance. The witnesses thus open a teaching not on dishonesty but on foresight: as the worldly steward provided shrewdly for his future, so the children of light must use their entrusted wealth for the world to come.
2:
On Luke 16:2 — “Give an account of thy stewardship; for thou mayest be no longer steward” — the witnesses mark the summons that hangs over every steward.

The grammatical-historical commentators read the demand. Barnes cautions that this is not directly “the day of judgment” but a circumstance “to prepare the way for what follows,” though all will indeed give account. Clarke renders it “give up the business of the stewardship” — produce the books. Ellicott hears in “How is it that I hear this of thee?” both “wonder… and indignation,” like the lord of the vineyard (Isaiah 5:4). The Reformed Gill reads the call of God through the prophets, John, Christ, and the apostles.

The verse strikes the note of accountability that runs through the parable. The steward’s tenure is ended; he must render his accounts and surrender his office. The wealth he managed was never his; and now the day comes when he must answer for his handling of it.

So the witnesses find in the master’s summons a picture of the reckoning to which all stewards come. Whether by the approach of death or the final judgement, every man must one day give account of the goods, the gifts, the opportunities God has lent him. The steward’s shock — that his stewardship is suddenly to be taken away — mirrors the rude awakening of those who have lived as though their possessions and their lives were their own. The witnesses note the mercy even in the warning: the steward is told before he is dismissed, and so given time to act. The verse thus presses on every reader the truth that we hold all in trust, and must render it up; the only wisdom is to use the brief remaining time of our stewardship well.

So the witnesses find in the master’s summons a picture of the reckoning to which all stewards come. Whether by death or the final judgment, every man must give account of the goods, gifts, and opportunities God has lent him. The steward’s shock — that his office is suddenly taken away — mirrors the rude awakening of those who lived as though their possessions were their own. The witnesses note the mercy even in the warning: he is told before he is dismissed, and so given time to act. We hold all in trust, and must render it up; the only wisdom is to use the brief remaining time well.
3:
On Luke 16:3 — “Then the steward said within himself, What shall I do?… I cannot dig; to beg I am ashamed” — the witnesses mark the steward’s anxious self-counsel.

The grammatical-historical commentators read the man’s plight. Barnes: he could not, or would not, “stoop to so low an employment” as daily labour; Robertson notes he “knew that he was guilty of embezzlement,” and that “to beg I am ashamed” betrays his false pride. Ellicott sharpens it: “he has lost the manliness… which would have fitted him for actual labour. He retains the false shame which makes him prefer fraud to poverty,” shuddering to “sit, like Lazarus, and ask alms.”

The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley fastens on the irony: “To beg I am ashamed — but not ashamed to cheat! This was likewise a sense of honour” — the honour “by men called honour, but by angels pride.”

So the witnesses lay bare the steward’s twisted reasoning, and through it the world’s. Faced with ruin, he will not work and is ashamed to beg, but is not ashamed to defraud his master afresh; his pride forbids honest poverty but not dishonest provision. The witnesses note, with Wesley, the perverse code of honour that finds begging shameful and cheating acceptable. Yet they also mark the one thing the steward did rightly — he faced his coming destitution and bestirred himself to provide for it. The verse thus holds up both a warning and a foil: a warning against the false shame that prefers fraud to labour, and a foil to the foresight the Lord will commend — the steward’s earnest reckoning with his future, which the careless soul, heedless of its own greater reckoning, so sadly lacks.

So the witnesses lay bare the steward’s twisted reasoning, and through it the world’s. Faced with ruin, he will not work and is ashamed to beg, but is not ashamed to defraud his master afresh; his pride forbids honest poverty but not dishonest provision. Wesley marks the perverse code — honour by men, pride by angels. Yet the witnesses also note the one thing he did rightly: he faced his coming destitution and bestirred himself to provide — a foil to the careless soul that, heedless of its greater reckoning, makes no such provision for eternity.
4:
On Luke 16:4 — “I am resolved what to do, that, when I am put out of the stewardship, they may receive me into their houses” — the witnesses mark the steward’s shrewd resolve.

The grammatical-historical commentators catch the moment of decision. Robertson renders the aorist (egnon) as “a burst of daylight to the puzzled, darkened man: I’ve got it, I see into it now, a sudden solution.” Barnes draws the lesson that “dishonesty will sooner or later lead us into difficulty,” and that the steward’s perplexity might have been spared by earlier honesty. Ellicott notes the “dramatic abruptness,” leaving “they” who would receive him to be supplied by the sequel.

The verse turns on the steward’s cunning. Unable to dig and ashamed to beg, he hits on a third way: to use his remaining authority to make friends among his master’s debtors, who will shelter him when his office is gone. His plan is dishonest, but it is provident; he secures his future by the very goods that are slipping from his hands.

So the witnesses mark the steward’s foresight as the point the Lord will press, even while his fraud is condemned. He looks beyond the immediate crisis to the time when he will be “put out,” and acts now, with what little power remains, to provide for then. The witnesses note the contrast the parable intends: the children of this world plan shrewdly for their earthly future, while the children of light, who have a greater future at stake, too often make no such provision. The steward’s “I am resolved” is the resolution of a man who reckons with what is coming; and the Lord holds it up, not for its dishonesty, but for the prudence that the heedless soul, drifting toward eternity without provision, would do well to imitate in better things.

So the witnesses mark the steward’s foresight as the point the Lord will press, even while his fraud is condemned. He looks beyond the crisis to the day he will be “put out,” and acts now to provide for then. The contrast the parable intends, the witnesses note, is plain: the children of this world plan shrewdly for their earthly future, while the children of light, with a greater future at stake, too often make no such provision. The steward’s “I am resolved” is the resolution of a man who reckons with what is coming — a prudence the heedless soul would do well to imitate in better things.
5:
On Luke 16:5 — “So he called every one of his lord’s debtors unto him, and said unto the first, How much owest thou unto my lord?” — the witnesses mark the steward’s scheme set in motion.

The voices here are brief. The grammatical-historical Barnes explains that, as steward, “he had the management of all the affairs, and… debts were to be paid to him”; the debtors were likely “tenants” who rented land of his master. Ellicott notes they might be buyers of produce or, “as the sequel renders more probable, tenants who… paid their rent in kind”; and asks who the “debtors” are in the interpretation, answering from the Lord’s Prayer — the debts we owe to God and to one another.

The verse begins the steward’s provident fraud. Summoning his master’s debtors one by one, he prepares to win their gratitude by lightening their bills — spending, while he still can, the goods entrusted to him, to secure friends for the day of his dismissal.

So the witnesses watch the steward act on his resolve with speed and address. He does not merely plan but executes; calling each debtor, he turns his master’s losses into his own future security. The witnesses, with Ellicott, look beneath the story to its lesson: in the interpretation, the “debtors” are those toward whom we may show the mercy that God’s mercy to us requires — the poor whose debts of need we can relieve, and so make friends against eternity. The steward’s dealing with his lord’s debtors becomes a parable of the believer’s dealing with God’s poor: using the wealth that is not finally ours, while we still have it, to lay up friendship and reward in the world to come.

So the witnesses watch the steward act on his resolve with speed and address. He does not merely plan but executes, turning his master’s losses into his own future security. Ellicott looks beneath the story: in the interpretation the “debtors” are those toward whom we may show the mercy God’s mercy to us requires — the poor whose debts of need we can relieve, and so make friends against eternity. The steward’s dealing becomes a parable of the believer’s: using the wealth that is not finally ours, while we still have it, to lay up friendship and reward in the world to come.
6:
On Luke 16:6 — “And he said, An hundred measures of oil. And he said… take thy bill, and sit down quickly, and write fifty” — the witnesses mark the first secret reduction.

The grammatical-historical commentators detail the measures and the haste. Barnes and Clarke identify the “measure” as the Hebrew bath — some seven and a half gallons of olive oil — a costly article of commerce (1 Kings 5:11). Robertson notes “thy bond” (the debtor’s written contract) and “quickly”: “it was a secret arrangement and speed was essential.” Lightfoot weighs why oil was abated by half but wheat by a fifth.

The verse shows the fraud in act. The steward bids the first debtor exchange his bond for a new one halving the debt — a swift, secret transaction that makes the debtor his grateful accomplice and friend. The “quickly” betrays the haste of a man working against the clock of his dismissal.

So the witnesses watch the steward buy friendship with his master’s property. By a stroke of the pen he wins a powerful obligation: the debtor, suddenly relieved of fifty measures, is bound to receive him when he is put out. The witnesses note the cunning — the steward gives away what is not his to secure what he needs — and the speed, for the secret must be done before his accounts are demanded. Yet the deeper lesson the parable presses is not the method but the motive of provision: the steward, knowing his office gone, makes friends with what he holds in trust against the coming day. The believer, the witnesses urge, is to do the like in righteousness — using the goods entrusted to him, while he holds them, to make friends who will welcome him into eternal habitations.

So the witnesses watch the steward buy friendship with his master’s property. By a stroke of the pen he wins a powerful obligation; the debtor, relieved of fifty measures, is bound to receive him. The “quickly” betrays a man working against the clock of his dismissal. Yet the deeper lesson is not the method but the motive of provision: knowing his office gone, he makes friends with what he holds in trust against the coming day. The believer, the witnesses urge, is to do the like in righteousness — using the goods entrusted to him to make friends who will welcome him into eternal habitations.
7:
On Luke 16:7 — “Then said he to another, And how much owest thou? And he said, An hundred measures of wheat. And he said… take thy bill, and write fourscore” — the witnesses mark the second reduction.

The grammatical-historical commentators detail the measure. Barnes and Clarke identify the “measure” here as the Hebrew cor (or homer), “the largest measure of capacity among the Hebrews,” some eight to ten bushels of wheat; Robertson notes it was “about ten bushels.” The reduction here is a fifth (a hundred to fourscore), where the oil was halved — a difference, Lightfoot conjectures, reflecting the relative values.

The verse repeats the fraud with a second debtor. Again the steward bids him rewrite his bond for less — a fifth struck off the wheat — binding another grateful friend to himself by the same stroke of his master’s loss.

So the witnesses see the steward multiply his future securities. With each debtor he lightens, he gains a new obligation and a new refuge for the day of his dismissal; the more friends, the surer his welcome. The witnesses note that the parable does not pause over the exact proportions of the reductions, which Calvin warns against pressing; the point is the steward’s settled policy of converting his master’s goods into personal goodwill. And the lesson, again, is the foresight, not the fraud: as the steward made many friends with the wealth passing from his hands, so the disciple is to use the “mammon of unrighteousness” — the money that is not finally his — in works of mercy that lay up friends and reward in heaven. The worldling’s shrewdness for earth rebukes the believer’s carelessness for eternity.

So the witnesses see the steward multiply his future securities. With each debtor he lightens, he gains a new refuge for the day of his dismissal. The parable does not pause over the exact proportions, which Calvin warns against pressing; the point is the steward’s settled policy of converting his master’s goods into goodwill. And the lesson, again, is the foresight, not the fraud: as he made many friends with the wealth passing from his hands, so the disciple is to use the “mammon of unrighteousness” in works of mercy that lay up friends and reward in heaven. The worldling’s shrewdness for earth rebukes the believer’s carelessness for eternity.
8:
On Luke 16:8 — “And the lord commended the unjust steward, because he had done wisely: for the children of this world are in their generation wiser than the children of light” — the witnesses labour to fix the sense of the praise.

The fathers and Reformers are at one that the praise is for prudence, not dishonesty. Augustine: “we ought not… to take the whole for our imitation” — not the fraud, but the foresight. The grammatical-historical commentators agree the “lord” is the steward’s master (not Christ), who “admires the sharpness… of the steward’s action” (Ellicott); “commended… not because he was unjust, but because he was wise” (Barnes). The Reformed Calvin and Gill insist God could not approve the injustice “contrary to his nature,” but the man’s “craft and cunning in providing” for his future.

The contrast that follows is the sting: “the children of this world are… wiser” in their own affairs than the children of light in theirs. Worldly men show, in pursuing earthly ends, a diligence and foresight that the godly too often lack in pursuing heavenly ones.

So the witnesses extract the lesson with care. The Lord does not commend the steward’s fraud — he calls him “the unjust steward” — but his providence: that, seeing his office ending, he acted decisively to secure his future. And he turns it into a rebuke of his own people: the men of the world are shrewder for time than the saints are for eternity, more earnest to provide for a few years on earth than believers are to provide for everlasting habitations. The witnesses thus read the parable’s point: not that we should imitate dishonesty, but that we should bring to the service of God and the use of wealth the same wakeful foresight that the worldling brings to his gain — making friends, by mercy, against the day when earthly stewardship ends.

So the witnesses extract the lesson with care. The Lord does not commend the steward’s fraud — he calls him “the unjust steward” — but his providence, that, seeing his office ending, he acted decisively. And he turns it into a rebuke of his own people: the men of the world are shrewder for time than the saints are for eternity, more earnest to provide for a few years on earth than believers are for everlasting habitations. The witnesses thus read the point: not that we imitate dishonesty, but that we bring to the service of God and the use of wealth the same wakeful foresight the worldling brings to his gain.
9:
On Luke 16:9 — “Make to yourselves friends of the mammon of unrighteousness; that, when ye fail, they may receive you into everlasting habitations” — the witnesses apply the parable.

The Latin and Eastern fathers read it of almsgiving. Gregory: “that after death they may find something in their own hand, let men before death place their riches in the hands of the poor.” Theophylact explains the “riches of unrighteousness” as the goods given for our brethren’s need which we wrongly spend on ourselves; Cyril, that Christ teaches the rich to “love the friendship of the poor, and to have treasure in heaven.” The grammatical-historical Robertson: “servants of God have to use it… for the kingdom… discreetly,” and it is “proper to make friends by the use of it.”

The Reformed Calvin guards the sense: Christ does not bid us seek human “defenders or advocates,” but teaches “that by acts of charity we obtain favour with God” (Psalm 18:25). Wesley: be “good stewards even of the lowest talents,” making friends “by doing all possible good, particularly to the children of God.”

So the witnesses draw the parable’s application. The believer is to use his money — called “the mammon of unrighteousness” because of the evil that so often attends its getting and using — not to hoard it for self, but to lay it out in mercy, making “friends” of the poor he relieves. Then, “when ye fail” (that is, at death), those friends, or the God who counts such mercy his own, will “receive” the giver into eternal habitations. The witnesses are careful, with Calvin, that this is no merit that purchases heaven, but the fruit of a faith that God rewards. The verse thus crowns the parable: as the steward made friends with his lord’s goods against his dismissal, so the disciple, with the wealth God lends, is to lay up by charity a welcome that abides when money fails.

So the witnesses draw the parable’s application. The believer is to use his money — the “mammon of unrighteousness,” so called for the evil that attends its getting and use — not to hoard for self but to lay out in mercy, making “friends” of the poor he relieves. Then, when he “fails” at death, those friends, or the God who counts such mercy his own, will receive him into eternal habitations. The witnesses are careful, with Calvin, that this is no merit purchasing heaven, but the fruit of a faith God rewards: charity lays up a welcome that abides when money fails.
10:
On Luke 16:10 — “He that is faithful in that which is least is faithful also in much: and he that is unjust in the least is unjust also in much” — the witnesses mark the maxim of fidelity.

The grammatical-historical commentators read it as a tested rule. Barnes: “a man that shows fidelity in small matters will also in large; and he that will cheat… in little things will also” in great. Robertson calls it “one of the profoundest sayings of Christ… The man who can be trusted in a very small thing will be promoted to large responsibilities… Men who embezzle in large sums began with small sums.” Clarke: by acting uprightly in little things one “acquires the gracious habit” of fidelity in great. Calvin notes these are “proverbs taken from ordinary practice,” generally though not invariably true.

The verse turns the parable toward its inward application. Money is “that which is least”; the “true riches” of grace and heaven are the much. Faithfulness or fraud in the handling of the small — earthly wealth — reveals and forms the character that will prove faithful or false in the great.

So the witnesses read in the maxim the link between the use of money and the state of the soul. The way a man handles the trifle of earthly goods is the index of his fidelity in the weighty matters of God; small dishonesties betray a heart that cannot be trusted with much. The witnesses, with Wesley, apply it to stewardship: “he that is faithful in what is meanest… worldly substance, is also faithful in things of a higher nature.” The verse thus dignifies the smallest stewardship: how we spend a coin is a test of the soul, and faithfulness in the little of money is the school and the proof of faithfulness in the much of grace. The unjust steward failed the test; the disciple is called to pass it.

So the witnesses read in the maxim the link between the use of money and the state of the soul. The way a man handles the trifle of earthly goods is the index of his fidelity in the weighty matters of God; small dishonesties betray a heart that cannot be trusted with much. Robertson calls it “one of the profoundest sayings of Christ.” The verse dignifies the smallest stewardship: how we spend a coin is a test of the soul, and faithfulness in the little of money is the school and proof of faithfulness in the much of grace.
11:
On Luke 16:11 — “If therefore ye have not been faithful in the unrighteous mammon, who will commit to your trust the true riches?” — the witnesses mark the contrast of the false and the true riches.

The grammatical-historical commentators draw out the “true riches.” Barnes: men who misuse money “cannot expect to grow in grace… God does not confer grace upon them.” Ellicott sets the “true riches” — “peace, pardon, wisdom,” or Paul’s “unsearchable riches of Christ” (Ephesians 3:8) — against “the vain, deceitful, unrighteous mammon.” Robertson warns soberly: “there is no sadder story than to see a preacher go down by the wrong use of money, caught in this snare of the devil.”

The verse presses the maxim of the last into a searching question. Money is the “unrighteous mammon” — the lesser, deceitful trust; the “true riches” are the spiritual treasures of God. If a man cannot be trusted with the lesser, how shall God commit to him the greater?

So the witnesses read a solemn warning to all who handle wealth, and especially to the servants of God. The right use of money is the proving-ground for the higher trust; unfaithfulness in the “unrighteous mammon” — covetousness, dishonesty, hoarding — forfeits the “true riches” of grace, which God will not commit to a heart enslaved to gold. The witnesses, with Lightfoot, instance Zacchaeus, who, though once unjust in scraping money, became “eminently faithful in… piously distributing it.” The verse thus binds the earthly and the heavenly stewardship together: the way we deal with the false riches now decides our fitness for the true riches hereafter; and the love of money, unmastered, shuts the soul out from the wealth that endures.

So the witnesses read a solemn warning to all who handle wealth, and especially the servants of God. The right use of money is the proving-ground for the higher trust; unfaithfulness in the “unrighteous mammon” forfeits the “true riches” of grace, which God will not commit to a heart enslaved to gold. The witnesses instance Zacchaeus, once unjust in scraping money, who became faithful in piously distributing it. The way we deal with the false riches now decides our fitness for the true riches hereafter; and the love of money, unmastered, shuts the soul out from the wealth that endures.
12:
On Luke 16:12 — “And if ye have not been faithful in that which is another man’s, who shall give you that which is your own?” — the witnesses mark the truth that earthly wealth is not our own.

The grammatical-historical commentators expound the contrast. Barnes: “the wealth of the world is” God’s, “committed to us as his stewards… still his”; the “your own” is the true and abiding inheritance. Robertson notes the textual variant (“your own” / “our own”) but the point is plain: “earthly wealth is ours as a loan, a trust, withdrawn at any moment… It belongs to another.” Ellicott: in relation to all external goods “we are stewards and not possessors.”

The Reformed Calvin draws the spiritual sense: the “your own” is what is truly “within man” — the heavenly riches that abide — for “God does not bestow riches upon us on condition that we shall be attached to them.” Wesley: “none of these temporal things are yours: you are only stewards… God is the proprietor of all.”

So the witnesses press the humbling truth that the wealth we call our own is another’s. It is God’s property, lent for a season, to be withdrawn at his pleasure and at death; we are stewards, never owners. The “your own,” by contrast, is the imperishable treasure of grace and glory — the inheritance that, once given, is truly and for ever ours. The witnesses thus complete the lesson of the steward: if we prove unfaithful with the goods that are merely entrusted, we forfeit the riches that would be our own possession. The verse rebukes the proud sense of ownership that grasps and hoards, and calls the disciple to hold his money loosely, as a trust to be used for God — that, faithful in another’s, he may receive his own.

So the witnesses press the humbling truth that the wealth we call our own is another’s — God’s property, lent for a season, withdrawn at his pleasure and at death; we are stewards, never owners. The “your own,” by contrast, is the imperishable treasure of grace and glory. If we prove unfaithful with goods merely entrusted, we forfeit the riches that would be our own possession. The verse rebukes the proud sense of ownership that grasps and hoards, and calls the disciple to hold his money loosely, as a trust used for God — that, faithful in another’s, he may receive his own.
13:
On Luke 16:13 — “No servant can serve two masters… Ye cannot serve God and mammon” — the witnesses mark the great either-or that closes the parable.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the saying and its place. Robertson observes it is “precisely the same” as Matthew 6:24, save the added “household servant” (oiketes), and that “there is no real reason for objecting to repetition of favourite sayings.” Clarke: “the heart will be either wholly taken up with God, or wholly engrossed with the world” (Joshua 24:15). Ellicott marks how it “took its place among the axioms of the religious life.”

The Reformed Wesley: “you cannot be faithful to God, if you trim between God and the world.” The Puritan Henry adds that when the Lord spoke thus, “the covetous Pharisees treated his instructions with contempt” — the very response the next verse records.

So the witnesses set the parable’s teaching under one sharp law: God and money cannot share the throne of the heart. A servant gives himself wholly to his master; and the soul that would serve God must not also serve mammon, for the two demand opposite things and a divided service is no service at all. The whole foregoing lesson — that we are stewards, that faithfulness in money proves the soul, that wealth is a trust to be used in mercy — rests on this: that money is to be the servant, never the master. The witnesses mark the searching application: not that a man may not have wealth, but that he may not serve it; and the covetous, who love money, are by this word excluded from the love and service of God. The verse thus draws the line the rest of the chapter will illustrate, between the Pharisee who served mammon and the disciple who serves God.

So the witnesses set the parable’s teaching under one sharp law: God and money cannot share the throne of the heart. A servant gives himself wholly to his master; the soul that would serve God must not also serve mammon, for the two demand opposite things and a divided service is none. The whole foregoing lesson rests on this — that money is to be the servant, never the master. The witnesses mark the application: not that a man may not have wealth, but that he may not serve it; and the covetous, who love money, are by this word excluded from the love and service of God.
14:
On Luke 16:14 — “And the Pharisees also, who were covetous, heard all these things: and they derided him” — the witnesses mark the scorn of the money-lovers.

The grammatical-historical commentators expose the motive of the mockery. Robertson notes “lovers of money” (philargyroi, only here and 2 Timothy 3:2), and that the imperfect “they scoffed” (exemyktrizon, literally “turned up the nose”) shows they had been “listening all the while” (verses 1–13). Barnes: they “contemned… the doctrine… because it showed them that with their love of money they could not be the true friends of God.” The Latin Bede: “they derided the Preacher of mercy, humility, and frugality.”

The Reformed Calvin marks the constancy of Christ against their mockery: he “does not give way to their disdainful conduct, but constantly maintains the authority of his doctrine” — a pattern for all ministers, to meet “ungodly despisers with the dreadful judgment of God.”

So the witnesses read the Pharisees’ derision as the self-betrayal of covetous hearts. The teaching that money is a trust, that none can serve God and mammon, struck at their ruling sin; and rather than repent, they sneered. The witnesses note the diagnosis the Evangelist supplies — they were “lovers of money” — and that their scorn was not of reason but of a guilty conscience that would not be touched. The verse thus turns the discourse from the disciples to the Pharisees, and prepares the stern words and the dread parable to follow. Their upturned noses at the gospel of frugality and mercy will be answered by the picture of a rich man who loved his ease and his purple, and lifted up his eyes in torment — the very end to which the love of money, derided as folly, leads.

So the witnesses read the Pharisees’ derision as the self-betrayal of covetous hearts. The teaching that money is a trust, that none can serve God and mammon, struck at their ruling sin; and rather than repent, they sneered. The Evangelist supplies the diagnosis — they were “lovers of money” — and their scorn was not of reason but of a guilty conscience. The verse turns the discourse from the disciples to the Pharisees, and prepares the dread parable to follow: their upturned noses at the gospel of mercy will be answered by the rich man who loved his purple and lifted up his eyes in torment.
15:
On Luke 16:15 — “Ye are they which justify yourselves before men; but God knoweth your hearts: for that which is highly esteemed among men is abomination in the sight of God” — the witnesses mark the rebuke of self-righteousness.

The grammatical-historical commentators read the exposure. Robertson: they were “past-masters” at self-justification, doing it “now by upturned noses”; and “abomination” (bdelygma) is the strong Old Testament word “for a detestable thing” — “withering scorn.” Ellicott links it to the parable of Luke 18:9–14: they forgot that “they stood before God as the Searcher of hearts.” Barnes notes the contrast of man’s sight and God’s knowledge (Jeremiah 17:10).

The Reformed Calvin: Christ “meets ungodly despisers with the dreadful judgment of God,” declaring that “the hypocrisy with which they deceive the eyes of men will be” exposed before God. Wesley sums the chain: “pride, wherewith you justify yourselves, feeds covetousness, derides the Gospel… and destroys the law.”

So the witnesses read the Lord’s answer to the Pharisees’ scorn. They justified themselves before men, parading a righteousness that covered a covetous heart; but God, who searches hearts, sees through the show. And the Lord lays down the great reversal: what men prize — wealth, station, the outward repute the Pharisees craved — is “abomination” to God. The witnesses note the force of the word: not merely inferior but detestable, the very opposite of God’s judgement. The verse thus strips the money-lovers of their defence: their esteem among men is no proof of standing with God, who weighs the heart and abhors the proud self-righteousness that the world admires. It prepares the way for the parable in which a man “highly esteemed” for his riches is found, in God’s sight, a lost soul in torment.

So the witnesses read the Lord’s answer to their scorn. They justified themselves before men, parading a righteousness that covered a covetous heart; but God, who searches hearts, sees through the show. And the Lord lays down the great reversal: what men prize — wealth, station, repute — is “abomination” (a withering word) to God. The verse strips the money-lovers of their defence: esteem among men is no proof of standing with God, who abhors the proud self-righteousness the world admires — and it prepares the parable in which a man “highly esteemed” for his riches is found, in God’s sight, a lost soul in torment.
16:
On Luke 16:16 — “The law and the prophets were until John: since that time the kingdom of God is preached, and every man presseth into it” — the witnesses mark the turning of the ages and the earnestness it demands.

The grammatical-historical commentators set it beside Matthew 11:12–13. Robertson notes the rare verb “entereth violently” (biazetai), and Lightfoot the two possible senses — either “every one breaketh into it by force” (the eager reception of the gospel, Beza), or “every one commits violence upon it” (its persecution, Vulgate). Ellicott: the Pharisees “had closed their eyes to the fact that all previous revelations led up to… John,” whose work was preparatory for Christ.

The Reformed Calvin reads the new openness of grace: God “is not as formerly seen at a distance under dark shadows, but appears openly and at hand”; Wesley, that “humble upright men receive it with inexpressible earnestness.”

So the witnesses read a declaration of the great change John’s coming marked. The law and the prophets held until him; with him the kingdom began to be preached, and now men press into it with eager force. The witnesses note the debate over “presseth” — whether the violence of zeal that storms the kingdom, or the violence of opposition that assails it — and incline, with most, to the former: the urgent, vehement earnestness with which the humble lay hold of the gospel. The verse rebukes the Pharisees from another side: while publicans and sinners pressed into the kingdom, they, the professed guardians of the law, stood outside, deriding. The witnesses thus set the eager faith of the lowly against the proud unbelief of the money-lovers, and prepare the warning that the law they boasted of would judge them.

So the witnesses read a declaration of the great change John’s coming marked: the law and prophets held until him; now the kingdom is preached, and men press into it with eager force. The witnesses note the debate over “presseth” — the violence of zeal that storms the kingdom, or of opposition that assails it — inclining, with most, to the former. The verse rebukes the Pharisees from another side: while publicans and sinners pressed in, they, the professed guardians of the law, stood outside, deriding — the eager faith of the lowly set against the proud unbelief of the money-lovers.
17:
On Luke 16:17 — “And it is easier for heaven and earth to pass, than one tittle of the law to fail” — the witnesses mark the abiding authority of the law.

The voices here are brief, referring to the fuller saying at Matthew 5:18. The Reformed Gill explains the purpose: lest any think that, the law and prophets being “until John,” they were now “abrogated and laid aside,” Christ affirms that “heaven and earth might sooner pass away” than the law fail. Barnes points to the like testimonies of the word’s permanence (Psalm 102:26; Isaiah 40:8). Ellicott notes the apparent lack of sequence, and that some links may be dropped, the verse guarding the gospel against the charge of lawlessness.

The verse balances the last. Though the law and prophets gave way, in their preparatory office, to the kingdom now preached, the law itself does not fail; not the smallest stroke of it shall pass. The gospel does not abolish the law but fulfils and upholds it.

So the witnesses guard the truth of the previous verse from misuse. The passing of the old dispensation into the new is no setting aside of God’s law, whose authority stands firmer than heaven and earth. The witnesses note the bearing on the Pharisees: so far from destroying the law, Christ honoured it more than they, who, while boasting of it, wrested and weakened it — as the next verse, on divorce, will show. The verse thus answers a double error: against the antinomian, that the gospel does not dissolve the law; against the self-righteous Pharisee, that his lax handling of the law was the real failing, while Christ upheld its full and unfailing authority. Not a tittle shall pass; and by that unfailing law the covetous despisers themselves shall be judged.

So the witnesses guard the previous verse from misuse. The passing of the old dispensation into the new is no setting aside of God’s law, whose authority stands firmer than heaven and earth. The bearing on the Pharisees is plain: so far from destroying the law, Christ honoured it more than they, who, while boasting of it, wrested and weakened it. The verse answers a double error — against the antinomian, that the gospel does not dissolve the law; against the self-righteous Pharisee, that his lax handling of the law was the real failing, while Christ upheld its full and unfailing authority. Not a tittle shall pass.
18:
On Luke 16:18 — “Whosoever putteth away his wife, and marrieth another, committeth adultery” — the witnesses read the example of how the Pharisees wrested the law.

The grammatical-historical commentators explain the connection. Barnes and Ellicott: the design is “to reprove the Pharisees for not observing the law… notwithstanding their great pretensions,” and to show “how they had really departed from” it — the “doctrine and discipline of divorce which the Pharisees taught, lowering the sacredness” of marriage. Robertson: “adultery remains adultery, divorce or no divorce.” The fuller treatment is at Matthew 5:32; Matthew 19:9.

The Latin Tertullian, against Marcion, marks that Christ “prohibits divorce… he makes it unlawful to marry a woman that has been put away,” where Moses had “permitted repudiation” (Deuteronomy 24:1) — Christ not contradicting the law but restoring its true intent.

So the witnesses read the saying on divorce as a chosen instance of the unfailing law just affirmed. The Pharisees, who boasted of the law, had in fact corrupted it, putting away their wives on every trifling pretext and so making light of marriage; Christ, far from abolishing the law, presses its sacred intent against their laxity. The witnesses note that the verse, set here among the rebukes of the money-lovers, illustrates the charge that they “destroy the law” while professing to keep it. It shows the Lord upholding the very commandment they evaded, and exposes the gap between their outward zeal for the law and their inward dishonouring of it. Not a tittle shall fail; and the marriage bond, loosed by the Pharisees, is bound fast again by the Christ who came to fulfil the law they had wrested.

So the witnesses read the saying on divorce as a chosen instance of the unfailing law just affirmed. The Pharisees, who boasted of the law, had corrupted it, putting away their wives on every trifling pretext and making light of marriage; Christ presses its sacred intent against their laxity. Set here among the rebukes of the money-lovers, it illustrates the charge that they “destroy the law” while professing to keep it. The marriage bond, loosed by the Pharisees, is bound fast again by the Christ who came to fulfil the law they had wrested.
19:
On Luke 16:19 — “There was a certain rich man, which was clothed in purple and fine linen, and fared sumptuously every day” — the witnesses open the account of the rich man and Lazarus.

The Latin and Eastern fathers read the rich man’s luxury and its peril. Chrysostom: “there was, not is, because he had passed away as a fleeting shadow.” Ambrose: “not all poverty is holy, or all riches criminal, but as luxury disgraces riches, so does holiness commend poverty.” Gregory marks that the Lord names the poor man but not the rich, “because God knows and approves the humble, but not the proud.” The grammatical-historical Robertson details the costly “purple” and “fine linen” of the wealthy; Bede, that the very sin is unmarked — the man is condemned not for fraud but for selfish luxury amid another’s want.

On whether this is parable or history the witnesses divide gently. Barnes, Gill, and Calvin take it a parable — “not… any particular case which had actually happened, but teaching that such cases might happen”; Clarke allows “either a parable or a real history.” Lightfoot mocks the friars who “point exactly to the place where” the rich man’s house stood.

So the witnesses open the most solemn of the Lord’s pictures of the life to come, set as the answer to the deriding money-lovers. The rich man is no robber; his sin is named in his daily purple and sumptuous feasting while a beggar starved at his gate — the sin of selfish wealth and pitiless ease. The Puritan Henry marks it: “a man may have a great deal of the wealth… of this world, yet perish for ever.” The witnesses note that the Lord withholds the rich man’s name but gives the poor man’s, reversing the world’s reckoning; and they prepare the great turn of the parable, where the man “highly esteemed” for his riches is, in God’s sight, the lost soul, and the despised beggar the heir of glory.

So the witnesses open the most solemn of the Lord’s pictures of the life to come, set as the answer to the deriding money-lovers. The rich man is no robber; his sin is named in his daily purple and sumptuous feasting while a beggar starved at his gate — the sin of selfish wealth and pitiless ease. Henry marks it: “a man may have a great deal of the wealth… of this world, yet perish for ever.” The Lord withholds the rich man’s name but gives the poor man’s, reversing the world’s reckoning — preparing the turn where the man esteemed for riches is the lost soul, and the despised beggar the heir of glory.
20:
On Luke 16:20 — “And there was a certain beggar named Lazarus, which was laid at his gate, full of sores” — the witnesses mark the poor man and his name.

The grammatical-historical commentators dwell on the one named figure. Barnes notes the original means simply “a poor man,” not “beggar,” and that “Lazarus” (Hebrew, “a man destitute of help” / “God a help”) suits his condition; Robertson, that “was laid” (ebebleto, past perfect) implies “he had been flung there,” with “contemptuous roughness.” Ellicott asks why this “one solitary instance of a personal name” in the parables, offering several answers; Clarke and Adam: the name is given “because his character was good, and his end glorious… the righteous shall be had in everlasting remembrance.”

The verse sets the beggar at the gate, in a misery as extreme as the rich man’s ease — destitute, diseased, “full of sores,” cast helpless where the feasting could be seen but not shared. The contrast is total: purple and rags, banquet and starvation, health and running sores, the gate between them.

So the witnesses read in the naming of Lazarus the reversal of the world’s values that the parable will press. The rich man, great in men’s eyes, is left nameless; the beggar, beneath men’s notice, is named before God — for, as Gregory said, God knows the humble. The witnesses note the pathos of the picture: a man so reduced that he is “flung” at another’s gate, his very name (“God a help”) his only comfort, his sores his daily portion. The gate that separated them is the parable’s silent accuser: the rich man had only to cross it to show mercy, and did not. The verse thus deepens the indictment of selfish wealth and lifts the despised poor into the light of God’s remembrance, where the last is first.

So the witnesses read in the naming of Lazarus the reversal of the world’s values the parable will press. The rich man, great in men’s eyes, is left nameless; the beggar, beneath men’s notice, is named before God, for God knows the humble. The witnesses note the pathos: a man so reduced he is “flung” at another’s gate, his name (“God a help”) his only comfort. The gate that separated them is the parable’s silent accuser: the rich man had only to cross it to show mercy, and did not. The verse lifts the despised poor into the light of God’s remembrance, where the last is first.
21:
On Luke 16:21 — “And desiring to be fed with the crumbs which fell from the rich man’s table: moreover the dogs came and licked his sores” — the witnesses mark the depth of Lazarus’ misery.

The grammatical-historical commentators read the two touches. Ellicott: the picture “is heightened” — “the dogs are there, and get the crumbs which the man fails to get,” and lick his sores; the Eastern want of knives made such waste larger (cf. Mark 7:28). Robertson notes the language recalls the prodigal (Luke 15:16); “probably he did” get the scraps, “though nothing more.” Clarke infers the rich man did not spurn him, since he later knew him by name.

The Reformed Calvin presses the rich man’s cruelty: “had there been a drop of humanity in him, he ought at least to have ordered a supply from his kitchen”; the crowning proof of his “worse than brutal disposition” was that “he did not learn pity even from” the dogs.

So the witnesses complete the portrait of the beggar’s wretchedness and the rich man’s heartlessness. Lazarus craves the very scraps that fall to the dogs; and the dogs, more merciful than the man, come and lick his sores. The witnesses note the silent indictment: the beasts show a pity the master does not, and the rich man, daily passing the sufferer at his own gate, is unmoved by a misery that moved the dogs. It is not that he robbed Lazarus, but that he ignored him — the sin of omission, of a wealth that feasts while need starves at the door. The verse thus seals the earthly scene before the great reversal: the rich man at ease, the beggar in extremity, the gate uncrossed by mercy — and prepares the day when the two estates shall be exchanged for ever.

So the witnesses complete the portrait of the beggar’s wretchedness and the rich man’s heartlessness. Lazarus craves the very scraps that fall to the dogs; and the dogs, more merciful than the man, lick his sores. The beasts show a pity the master does not; the rich man, daily passing the sufferer at his own gate, is unmoved by a misery that moved the dogs. It is not that he robbed Lazarus but that he ignored him — the sin of omission, of a wealth that feasts while need starves at the door. The earthly scene is sealed before the great reversal.
22:
On Luke 16:22 — “And it came to pass, that the beggar died, and was carried by the angels into Abraham’s bosom: the rich man also died, and was buried” — the witnesses mark the great reversal at death.

The fathers dwell on the two ends. Pseudo-Chrysostom: “the one angels receive, the other torments… those great sufferings are suddenly exchanged for bliss.” Gregory: God “by trials exercised the poor man to glory, by endurance awaited the rich man to punishment.” Gregory of Nyssa: the just judgment of God is “a mirror” answering our dispositions, the merciless man finding no mercy. Augustine warns against taking “Abraham’s bosom” as anything “corporeal.” The grammatical-historical Robertson and Ellicott note “Abraham’s bosom” as the popular Jewish image of the blessed feast; Barnes, the angels bearing the righteous, taken (as the burial) literally.

The verse turns the whole picture about. Both men die — the common lot; but their estates are reversed. The beggar, unburied perhaps and unmourned, is borne by angels to bliss; the rich man, buried with pomp, finds his pomp end at the grave. Death, which the rich man thought far off, levels and then exalts and abases.

So the witnesses read in the double death the hinge of eternity. The gate that had separated purple from rags now separates bliss from torment, and the separation is everlasting and reversed: Lazarus, who had nothing, is carried to Abraham’s bosom; the rich man, who had all, is “buried” — and, as the next verse shows, lifts up his eyes in hell. The witnesses note the angels’ ministry to the poor saint, and the silence over the rich man’s soul, marked only by his funeral. The verse thus answers the deriding money-lovers with the end of their own road: that the world’s esteem reaches only to the grave, while God’s reckoning reaches beyond it — and the despised believer, borne by angels, inherits the joy the rich man scorned.

So the witnesses read in the double death the hinge of eternity. The gate that separated purple from rags now separates bliss from torment, and the separation is everlasting and reversed: Lazarus, who had nothing, is carried by angels to Abraham’s bosom; the rich man, who had all, is “buried” — and lifts up his eyes in hell. The witnesses note the angels’ ministry to the poor saint and the silence over the rich man’s soul, marked only by his funeral. The verse answers the deriding money-lovers with the end of their own road: the world’s esteem reaches only to the grave, while God’s reckoning reaches beyond it.
23:
On Luke 16:23 — “And in hell he lift up his eyes, being in torments, and seeth Abraham afar off, and Lazarus in his bosom” — the witnesses mark the rich man’s torment and sight.

The grammatical-historical commentators define the place. Ellicott and Robertson note the word is Hades, “the unseen world of the dead,” not Gehenna — an earlier stage than the final judgement; “in torments” (basanois, the touchstone, then the rack). Barnes lists the circumstances: “far off from the abodes of the righteous” — “Lazarus was seen afar off” — “a place of torment.” The Reformed Calvin cautions that “Christ… describes spiritual things under figures… adapted to our senses”; souls have no bodily eyes or tongue, but the parable paints “the condition of the life to come.”

The verse opens the scene beyond death. The rich man, lately at ease, now lifts his eyes in torment, and sees — across an impassable distance — the bliss he has lost and the beggar he despised now comforted in Abraham’s bosom. His punishment is sharpened by the sight.

So the witnesses read the terrible awakening of the rich man. Chrysostom marks that, as the sight of the rich man’s feast once aggravated the beggar’s want, so now the sight of Lazarus’ bliss aggravates the rich man’s torment. The witnesses are careful, with Calvin, not to press the bodily details — the eyes, the seeing — as if souls had flesh, but to take the parable’s plain teaching: that there is, after death, a real and conscious state of torment for the lost and bliss for the saved, and that the two are within sight yet for ever apart. The verse answers the money-lovers’ scorn with a glimpse of their own end: the man who would not look across his gate at Lazarus must now look across the gulf of eternity at the joy he forfeited — remembering, seeing, and tormented.

So the witnesses read the terrible awakening of the rich man. Chrysostom marks that, as the sight of the feast once aggravated the beggar’s want, so now the sight of Lazarus’ bliss aggravates the rich man’s torment. The witnesses are careful, with Calvin, not to press the bodily details as if souls had flesh, but to take the plain teaching: there is, after death, a real and conscious state of torment for the lost and bliss for the saved, within sight yet for ever apart. The man who would not look across his gate at Lazarus must now look across the gulf of eternity at the joy he forfeited.
24:
On Luke 16:24 — “Father Abraham, have mercy on me, and send Lazarus, that he may dip the tip of his finger in water, and cool my tongue; for I am tormented in this flame” — the witnesses mark the rich man’s plea.

The grammatical-historical commentators read the cry. Barnes notes he still clings to his descent — “Father Abraham” — the very ground of Jewish pride; Ellicott sees in “send Lazarus” “the old selfish arrogance… who still looks on Lazarus as one who may be sent hither and thither”; Robertson notes the small mercy begged — “the tip of his finger… to cool” — from the man who denied the beggar even crumbs.

The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley turns to the doctrinal abuse: “here is one precedent in Scripture of praying to departed saints: but who is it that prays, and with what success?” — a damned soul, and in vain.

So the witnesses read the rich man’s plea as a study in too-late repentance and unaltered character. He cries for mercy and for the least relief — a drop of water on a burning tongue — and still treats Lazarus as a servant to be sent on his errands. The witnesses note the just reversal: he who would not give crumbs now begs a drop, and is refused; the beggar at his gate is now beyond his command. And the witnesses mark the anti-Catholic point Wesley presses: that this solitary instance of address to a departed saint is no warrant for prayer to the saints, being the desperate, fruitless cry of a lost soul, not the worship of the Church. The verse thus shows torment without relief and pride without repentance — the awful fixity of the state beyond death.

So the witnesses read the rich man’s plea as a study in too-late repentance and unaltered character. He cries for the least relief — a drop of water on a burning tongue — and still treats Lazarus as a servant to be sent on his errands. The just reversal is plain: he who would not give crumbs now begs a drop, and is refused. And the witnesses mark Wesley’s point, that this lone instance of address to a departed saint is no warrant for prayer to the saints, being the fruitless cry of a lost soul, not the worship of the Church.
25:
On Luke 16:25 — “But Abraham said, Son, remember that thou in thy lifetime receivedst thy good things, and likewise Lazarus evil things: but now he is comforted, and thou art tormented” — the witnesses mark the answer of memory and reversal.

The grammatical-historical commentators weigh the address and the word. Ellicott notes Abraham does not disown the relationship — “Son” — yet the word is, Barnes says, “a cutting word in this place,” and “Remember” one of the keenest, for memory itself is part of the torment. Robertson: “receivedst” (apelabes) means he got “in full” his chosen portion — “thy good things,” but “the evil things” (not Lazarus’s own). Calvin reads “Son” as “ironical,” a reproof to one who boasted his descent from Abraham.

The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley draws the point: “Thou didst choose and accept of worldly things as thy good, thy happiness. And can any be at a loss to know why he was in torments?”

So the witnesses read Abraham’s answer as the verdict of a just reversal grounded in the men’s own choices. The rich man had his “good things” — the good he chose, the comfort he made his portion — and is now without them; Lazarus had his evil things, and is now comforted. The witnesses note the force of “Remember”: the lost retain their memory, and the recollection of mercies abused and a portion misspent is itself a torment. They mark, too, that the rich man is condemned not for being rich but for making earthly good his “good,” his happiness and his all, with no thought of God or the poor. The verse thus lays bare the root of his ruin — a heart that chose the world for its portion — and the equity of the reversal: he received his reward, and it is spent.

So the witnesses read Abraham’s answer as the verdict of a just reversal grounded in the men’s own choices. The rich man had his “good things” — the good he chose, the comfort he made his portion — and is now without them; Lazarus had his evil things, and is comforted. The force of “Remember” is keen: the lost retain memory, and the recollection of mercies abused is itself a torment. He is condemned not for being rich but for making earthly good his “good,” his all, with no thought of God or the poor — the root of his ruin, and the equity of the reversal.
26:
On Luke 16:26 — “And beside all this, between us and you there is a great gulf fixed: so that they which would pass from hence to you cannot” — the witnesses mark the impassable and permanent separation.

The grammatical-historical commentators stress the fixity. Barnes: the “gulf” (chasm) is “fixed… strengthened, made firm or immovable… It will for ever divide heaven and hell.” Robertson notes the perfect passive “is fixed” (esteriktai) — “a permanent chasm… there on purpose.” Ellicott pictures it like “one of the… pictures of Dante’s Commedia,” the flames on one side and Paradise on the other. The Reformed Calvin: the words “describe the permanency of the future state… the boundaries… can never be broken through,” warning us to “return early to the path… lest we rush headlong into that abyss.”

The verse adds to the reversal its dreadful finality. Beyond the contrast of bliss and torment lies a fixed gulf that none can cross — no passage from the lost to the saved, nor from the saved to the lost. The states are not only opposite but eternal and sealed.

So the witnesses read in the “great gulf” the doctrine of the irreversibility of the eternal state. There is no purgatory here, no second chance, no traffic between the two abodes; what death fixes, eternity keeps. The witnesses note, with Calvin, the practical urgency: precisely because the gulf is fixed hereafter, the time to turn is now, while the way is open. They mark, too, that the impossibility cuts both ways — the rich man cannot be relieved, and Lazarus cannot be sent — so that the parable holds out no hope of remedy after death. The verse thus seals the warning to the money-lovers: the choices made in life are ratified for ever beyond it, and the gulf they will not cross to mercy now becomes the gulf that none can cross then.

So the witnesses read in the “great gulf” the irreversibility of the eternal state. There is no purgatory here, no second chance, no traffic between the two abodes; what death fixes, eternity keeps. The witnesses note, with Calvin, the practical urgency: precisely because the gulf is fixed hereafter, the time to turn is now, while the way is open. The impossibility cuts both ways — the rich man cannot be relieved, Lazarus cannot be sent — so the parable holds out no remedy after death. The choices made in life are ratified for ever beyond it.
27:
On Luke 16:27 — “Then he said, I pray thee therefore, father, that thou wouldest send him to my father’s house” — the witnesses mark the rich man’s turn to his living brethren.

The Latin and Eastern fathers read the new petition. Gregory: “when the rich man… found that all hope was taken away from him, his mind turns to those relations whom he had left”; and he notes the dreadful aggravation, that “his knowledge and memory are preserved” — “he knew Lazarus whom he despised, he remembered his brethren.” Augustine raises the question how the dead can care for the living, answering that the rich man might wish it without knowing “what they were doing.” Chrysostom: their care belongs more to God, who “appointed them teachers” in Moses and the prophets.

The grammatical-historical Robertson notes the Roman Catholic appeal to this petition for prayer to saints, observing that “both are in Hades” and that “Abraham makes no effort to communicate with the five brothers.”

So the witnesses read the rich man’s second plea as a mingling of awakened feeling and abiding helplessness. Finding no relief for himself, he thinks of his five brothers, lest they share his doom — a stirring, the fathers note, of concern that came too late and could effect nothing. The witnesses mark the lessons drawn: that the lost retain memory and knowledge, which deepens their torment; that, as Calvin and Robertson press, the Roman inference of intercession to departed saints is unfounded, for the parable shows no commerce between the dead and the living, nor any answered request. The verse thus turns the parable toward its final point — the sufficiency of Scripture to warn — and shows the rich man, even in his belated care for his brethren, unable to reach across the gulf he had fixed by his own life.

So the witnesses read the rich man’s second plea as a mingling of awakened feeling and abiding helplessness. Finding no relief for himself, he thinks of his five brothers — a concern, the fathers note, that came too late and could effect nothing. The witnesses mark the lessons: that the lost retain memory and knowledge, which deepens their torment; and that, as Calvin and Robertson press, the Roman inference of intercession to departed saints is unfounded, for the parable shows no commerce between the dead and the living, nor any answered request — the rich man unable to reach across the gulf he had fixed by his own life.
28:
On Luke 16:28 — “For I have five brethren; that he may testify unto them, lest they also come into this place of torment” — the witnesses weigh the rich man’s motive.

The voices here are brief. The grammatical-historical Robertson: the rich man “labours under the delusion that his five brothers will believe the testimony of Lazarus as a man from the dead.” Ellicott weighs the motive — was it “a selfish fear of reproaches that might aggravate his sufferings,” or “the stirring in him of an unselfish anxiety”? — and inclines, charitably, to the latter. Wesley notes he “might justly fear lest their reproaches should add to his own torment.”

The verse gives the substance of the rich man’s request: that his brothers, who live as he had lived, be warned by a messenger from the dead before they come to his place of torment. He desires for them the warning he had not heeded.

So the witnesses read the petition with a mixture of pity and discernment. There is, perhaps, a first stirring of unselfish concern — he would spare his brethren his doom — though some find even here a fear for his own added torment. Either way, the witnesses note, his proposed remedy is mistaken: he trusts a portent, a ghost from the grave, where God has given the surer witness of Scripture. The five brethren stand for all who live in the same love of money and ease, presuming on their descent from Abraham; and the rich man’s belated wish to warn them measures how late and how vain his awakening is. The verse thus turns to the great question the parable will answer: what will truly warn the living — a wonder, or the word of God?

So the witnesses read the petition with a mixture of pity and discernment. There is, perhaps, a first stirring of unselfish concern — he would spare his brethren his doom — though some find even here a fear for his own added torment. Either way, his proposed remedy is mistaken: he trusts a portent, a ghost from the grave, where God has given the surer witness of Scripture. The five brethren stand for all who live in the same love of money and ease; and his belated wish to warn them measures how late and how vain his awakening is.
29:
On Luke 16:29 — “Abraham saith unto him, They have Moses and the prophets; let them hear them” — the witnesses mark the sufficiency of Scripture.

The grammatical-historical commentators expound the answer. Barnes: “Moses” is the first five books, “the prophets” the rest of the Old Testament; “hear them… read them, or hear them read in the synagogues.” Clarke: this “supposes they were all Jewish believers… they had these writings… but did not permit them to influence their lives.” Ellicott: “the right use of lower knowledge is the condition of attaining to the higher,” for “he that hath, to him shall be given” (John 5:39 on searching the Scriptures).

The Reformed Calvin draws the doctrine: “we have received an undoubted rule of life, and… we have no right to expect that the dead will rise to instruct” us; the Scriptures, given for posterity, are God’s appointed and sufficient witness.

So the witnesses read Abraham’s reply as the parable’s great affirmation of the word of God. The brethren do not lack warning; they have Moses and the prophets, the very Scriptures that testify of duty, of judgement, and of mercy. The remedy the rich man craved — a messenger from the dead — is unnecessary, for God has already spoken sufficiently. The witnesses note the rebuke implied to the Pharisees, who “had” the Scriptures and boasted of them, yet “did not permit them to influence their lives”; and the abiding lesson for all — that the ordinary means of grace, the reading and hearing of God’s word, are God’s appointed way to repentance, and that those who will not heed the Scriptures would not be saved by signs. “Let them hear them”: the word in their hands is enough, if they will hear.

So the witnesses read Abraham’s reply as the parable’s great affirmation of the word of God. The brethren do not lack warning; they have Moses and the prophets, the Scriptures that testify of duty, judgment, and mercy. The remedy the rich man craved — a messenger from the dead — is unnecessary, for God has already spoken sufficiently. The witnesses note the rebuke to the Pharisees, who “had” the Scriptures yet let them not influence their lives; and the abiding lesson: the ordinary reading and hearing of God’s word is his appointed way to repentance, and those who will not heed it would not be saved by signs.
30:
On Luke 16:30 — “And he said, Nay, father Abraham: but if one went unto them from the dead, they will repent” — the witnesses mark the craving for a sign.

The grammatical-historical commentators read the plea. Barnes: he urges that the novelty of “one… from the dead… would attract their attention.” Robertson notes the rich man now sees the one thing lacking — not wealth or alms but “repentance”; he had thought repentance “was for others, not for all.” Ellicott sets it in “the general Jewish craving for a ‘sign’” (1 Corinthians 1:22) as the only proof of a revelation.

The Reformed Calvin reads it of the living more than the dead: it expresses “the feelings of the living… some would desire that angels should descend… others, that the dead should come out of their graves” — the perpetual demand for wonders in place of the word.

So the witnesses read the rich man’s “Nay” as the voice of unbelief that always prefers a sign to the Scriptures. He knows his brethren — a hardened people who, he thinks, will not hear Moses, but would be startled by a ghost from the grave. The witnesses note the deep error: that men suppose a marvel would convert where the word has failed, when in truth the heart that resists God’s plain word would resist his wonders too. They mark, with Robertson, the rich man’s late discovery that repentance was the one thing needful, for himself as for all. The verse thus voices the standing demand of unbelief — “show us a sign” — and prepares Abraham’s final, decisive answer, that the refusal to hear the Scriptures is a refusal that no resurrection would cure.

So the witnesses read the rich man’s “Nay” as the voice of unbelief that always prefers a sign to the Scriptures. He knows his brethren — a hardened people who, he thinks, would be startled by a ghost though they will not hear Moses. The deep error, the witnesses note, is the supposition that a marvel would convert where the word has failed, when the heart that resists God’s plain word would resist his wonders too. They mark, with Robertson, his late discovery that repentance was the one thing needful, for himself as for all — and prepare Abraham’s final, decisive answer.
31:
On Luke 16:31 — “If they hear not Moses and the prophets, neither will they be persuaded, though one rose from the dead” — the witnesses mark the parable’s solemn conclusion.

The grammatical-historical commentators read its force and its fulfilment. Barnes draws the lessons: the soul lives on after death, conscious; the Scriptures are God’s sufficient warning. Clarke: the sacred writings contain “such proofs of a Divine origin” that no risen dead could give greater. Lightfoot and Ellicott point to the striking fulfilment — that the raising of another Lazarus (John 11:43) did not persuade the Pharisees but hardened them to plot his death, and that Christ’s own resurrection left the unbelieving unbelieving still. Robertson: “a sharp discouragement against efforts… to communicate with the dead.”

The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley presses that true repentance “implies an entire change of heart: but a thousand apparitions cannot effect this. God only can, applying his word.”

So the witnesses close the parable, and the chapter, on its great verdict: the heart that will not hear the word of God would not be turned by a miracle. The fault lies not in the want of evidence but in the will that resists it; men who despise the Scriptures they possess would explain away the dead themselves. The witnesses note the dread confirmation in history — that the resurrection of Lazarus of Bethany only sharpened the rulers’ murderous unbelief, and the Lord’s own rising did not melt the hardened. The verse thus answers the money-lovers and all who crave a sign: God’s appointed means is his word, and to refuse it is to refuse the one thing that can save. Repentance comes not by wonders but by hearing Moses, the prophets, and the gospel they foreshadowed — and the soul that will not hear is left, like the rich man, beyond the gulf, with its warning unheeded and its choice sealed.

So the witnesses close the parable, and the chapter, on its great verdict: the heart that will not hear the word of God would not be turned by a miracle. The fault lies not in the want of evidence but in the will that resists it. The witnesses note the dread confirmation in history — that the raising of Lazarus of Bethany ({r("John 11:43","John 11:43")}) only sharpened the rulers’ murderous unbelief, and the Lord’s own rising did not melt the hardened. God’s appointed means is his word, and to refuse it is to refuse the one thing that can save: repentance comes not by wonders but by hearing Moses, the prophets, and the gospel they foreshadowed.
